title: Kids Flip Chair / Toddler Flip Seat
summary: A toddler chair that flips over to give two seat heights — one low, one higher — from the same little box. Five boards and a countersink bit.
description:
A clever two-in-one toddler seat: a small open box with a seat panel set off-center, so flipping it over gives you two different seat heights from one chair. It grows a little with the kid, and it's about the simplest useful thing you can build for a toddler.

The whole design hinges on one layout — the 'T' shape where the seat meets the two backs. Use one of your boards to lightly draw that T and locate the screw holes, then drill evenly spaced, countersunk holes on both sides so the screws sink flush and fill clean. The maker's tip is worth keeping: glue the seat-to-back joints so you have fewer holes to fill, and only add pocket-hole screws there if you actually enjoy filling and sanding.

Cut the two side boards together so they end up exactly the same — measure your actual boards clamped as a pair and cut to that number, because nominal 1x10 varies. Then round every corner and edge generously: this is furniture a small child climbs on, and a no-VOC paint keeps it safe to chew on, which toddlers will.

tools.note: Countersink bit so the screw heads sink flush and fill.
Square crosscuts; a hand saw works for so few cuts.
Round every corner — a toddler climbs on this.

cutList.note: Measure both boards together and cut to that exact number so they're flush (~13-11/16").

steps.title: Cut the sides, seat, and backs
steps.body: From the 4 ft 1x10, cut the two sides and the seat. Clamp the two side pieces together, measure them as a pair, and cut both to the same length: nominally 13 3/4", though real 1x10 varies, and what matters is that the two match exactly. Cut the seat 14" long from the same board; a 1x10 is 9 1/4" wide, so the sides and seat are all 9 1/4" deep. Cut the wide back 14" long from the 1x8 (7 1/4" wide) and the narrow back 14" long from the 1x6 (5 1/2" wide). Every crosscut wants to be square, because this chair is a small box and an out-of-square end is what makes it rock on a hard floor.

steps.title: Lay out and drill the screw holes
steps.body: Stand the three 14" boards on edge against one side piece and draw the T they form. Stacked up the 13 3/4" height, the narrow back (5 1/2"), the seat (3/4"), and the wide back (7 1/4") come to 13 1/2", so marking the seat about 5 1/2" up from one edge puts it about 7 1/2" up from the other. That difference is exactly the two seat heights the flip gives you. Carry the same marks onto the second side so both ends are identical, then mark two evenly spaced screw locations inside each board's outline and drill them with the countersink bit so every head sinks below the surface and fills clean.

steps.title: Assemble the seat and backs
steps.body: Lay one side flat, run glue on the end of each 14" board, and drive 1-3/4" screws through the side into the seat and the two back boards at the countersunk holes; then lay the second side on and repeat. Glue the joints where the two back boards meet the seat's long edges and rely on the glue there, so you have fewer holes to fill, adding pocket-hole screws only if you do not mind the extra filling and sanding. Because the seat sits off-centre rather than halfway up, the chair gives one seat height as built and the other when you turn it over. Check the assembly for square as you go: the three 14" boards are what hold the two sides parallel.

steps.title: Sand, finish, and flip
steps.body: Fill every countersunk hole, let it dry, then sand the whole chair and round the corners and edges generously, well past a light easing, because a toddler will land on them. Sit the chair on the floor both ways up and check it does not rock; a rock here usually means one side was cut a hair out of square, and it is easier to sand out now than later. Finish with the no-VOC paint, since toddlers chew furniture, and let it cure fully before the chair goes into use.

images.alt: Toddler flip chair that gives two seat heights depending on which way up it sits
